---
title: "Example Workflow"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{castform-example-workflow}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

Below is an example of a typical end-to-end workflow using the castform package 
to analyze historic weather station data from Prince Edward Island, Canada. 

```{r}
#| message: false
library(castform)
```

## (1) Download the latest station inventory

Make sure that we are working with the most up-to-date station inventory list 
from Environment Canada

```{r, eval = FALSE}
#| message: false
get_metadata()
```

## (2) Look up the stations available in Prince Edward Island

Summarize stations in our province of interest to ensure that there is enough 
data to complete an analysis on. 

```{r}
#| message: false
PE_stations <- station_lookup(province = "prince edward island")

c("Number of stations" = nrow(PE_stations), 
  "Earliest Collection Year" = min(PE_stations$HLY.First.Year), 
  "Latest Collection Year" = max(PE_stations$HLY.Last.Year))
```

From the station look-up, there are 83 weather stations in Nova Scotia with data 
available in varying ranges from 1953 to 2026.

## (3) Test Download with One File

Before committing, we will test our downloads by downloading the first month of available 
data from the first station (Station Name = "`r PE_stations$stationName[1]`" ; 
Station ID = `r PE_stations$Station.ID[1]`)

```{r, eval = FALSE}
#| message: false
get_single_station_file(station_id = 7103,
                        root_folder = "castform_outputs")
```

## (4) Download Province Stations

Now that we ensure the download works and creates the directory in the correct 
place, we can move onto the pull province download. 

```{r, eval = FALSE}
#| message: false
province_station_files(province = "prince edward island",
                       root_folder = "castform_outputs")
```

## (5) Create the Database

Next, we can create an sqlite database to store downloaded data and allow for 
analysis through queries.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
build_station_database(db_name = "PEI",
                       root_folder = "castform_outputs/PRINCE_EDWARD_ISLAND,
                       output_dir = "castform_outputs")
```

Before continuing, we must validate the created database (`NS_database.sqlite`) 
to ensure that the data is stored with the proper schema. 

```{r}
validate_database(db_name = "PEI",
                  db_dir = "castform_outputs")
```

## (6) Exploratory Data Analysis

Now we can explore the downloaded data.

### (6a) Station Map

First, we will create a map to visualize the stations we are working with. 

```{r, eval = FALSE}
#| message: false

station_map(db_name = "PEI",
            db_dir = "castform_outputs",
            output_dir = "castform_outputs",
            metadata_stations = FALSE)
```

```{r, echo = FALSE}
#| out.width: "70%"
#| fig.align: "center"
knitr::include_graphics("castform_outputs/PEI_outputs/PEI_STATION_MAP.png")
```

### (6b) Data Missingness

Next, we can create a table that summarizes how much data is missing within the 
variables of each station. 

```{r, eval = FALSE}
data_missingness_table(db_name = "PEI",
                      db_dir = "castform_outputs",
                      output_dir = "castform_outputs",
                      write_csv = TRUE)
```

```{r, echo = FALSE}
#| message: false
#| out.width: "100%"
#| fig.align: "center"
knitr::include_graphics("castform_outputs/PEI_outputs/Example_Data_Missingness_Table.png")
```

### (6c) Data Ranges

Next, we can create a table that summarizes how much data is average, minimum, 
and maximum values of each variable of each station. 

```{r, eval = FALSE}
data_ranges(db_name = "PEI",
            db_dir = "castform_outputs",
            output_dir = "castform_outputs/PEI_outputs",
            write_csv = TRUE)
```

```{r, echo = FALSE}
#| message: false
#| out.width: "100%"
#| fig.align: "center"
knitr::include_graphics("castform_outputs/PEI_outputs/Example_Data_Ranges_Table.png")
```

### (6f) Visualize Yearly Means

Next, we can create a plot that visualizes the means of each station vairable 
over time. This can also help identify which years of data are missing in each 
station. 

```{r, eval = FALSE}
plot_yearly_means(db_name = "PEI",
                  db_dir = "castform_outputs",
                  output_dir = "castform_outputs",
                  write_csv = TRUE)
```

```{r, echo = FALSE}
#| message: false
#| out.width: "100%"
#| fig.align: "center"
knitr::include_graphics("castform_outputs/PEI_outputs/Example_Yearly_Means_Plot.png")
```

### (6e) Visualize Missing Strings

Next, we can create a table and plot that identifies when strings of missing 
variable data occurred. This allows us to see exactly what hour the missing data 
starts and see how long it was missing for. 

*NOTE:* This output takes long to load on a large dataset so it was run on a subset 
of the database (only one station)

```{r, eval = FALSE}
pull_missing_strings(db_name = "PEI",
                    db_dir = "castform_outputs",
                    output_dir = "castform_outputs",
                    write_csv = TRUE)
```

```{r, echo = FALSE}
#| message: false
#| out.width: "100%"
#| fig.align: "center"
knitr::include_graphics("castform_outputs/PEI_outputs/Example_Missing_Strings_Plot.png")
```

### (6f) Visualize Repeated Strings

Last, we can create a table and plot that identifies when strings of repeated and 
identical data strings occurred. This allows us to see exactly what hour the 
repetition starts and see how long it lasts. This helps identify faulty machinery 
that may impact that reliability of our data. 

*NOTE:* This output takes long to load on a large dataset so it was run on a subset 
of the database (only one station)

```{r, eval = FALSE}
pull_repeated_strings(db_name = "PEI",
                      db_dir = "castform_outputs",
                      output_dir = "castform_outputs",
                      write_csv = TRUE)
```

```{r, echo = FALSE}
#| message: false
#| out.width: "100%"
#| fig.align: "center"
knitr::include_graphics("castform_outputs/PEI_outputs/Example_Repeated_Strings_Plot.png")
```

## (7) Detect Heat Waves 

After we have explored our data,  understand its organization, and are aware of
its flaws, we can use it to detect extreme heat wave events in historical data.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
heatwave_detector(db_name = "PEI",
                  db_dir = "castform_outputs",
                  output_dir = "castform_outputs",
                  write_csv = TRUE)
```

```{r, echo = FALSE}
#| message: false
#| out.width: "100%"
#| fig.align: "center"
knitr::include_graphics("castform_outputs/PEI_outputs/Example_HeatWave.png")
```
